content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This level of operational detail is what I cover every week in The Revenue Signal: the AI signals reshaping B2B revenue, the systems winning companies are building, and one concrete move per issue. Free, one email per week: https://revenueexperts.ai/ai-roi-scorecard/
Ask executives at established companies which AI project they want first, and workflow automation usually tops the list. It is the project with the most intuitive business case: take a process people do by hand, let software do it, bank the hours.
It is also the project most likely to be quietly abandoned a few months later.
The reason is rarely the technology. The reason is the order of operations. Automation was asked to run on top of scattered data and undefined processes, and it did exactly what automation does with inputs like that.
Automation multiplies what it is given
Press enter or click to view image in full size

An automation does not understand your business. It executes a definition of your business. If the process is well defined and the data underneath it is organized, the automation executes value at speed. If the process lives half in a spreadsheet and half in one employee’s habits, and the data sits in six systems that disagree with each other, the automation executes that instead.
Automation does not fix a process. It accelerates it.
Automating disorganized data and undefined processes produces faster chaos: wrong records propagated instantly, exceptions nobody mapped now failing at machine speed, and a team that trusts the system less every week. The manual version at least had a person catching the nonsense. The automated version ships the nonsense with a timestamp.
The most common failed attempt executives describe to us follows this exact shape: the company tried to automate workflows before organizing the company data those workflows depend on. The pilot demo worked, because demos run on prepared inputs. The rollout met the real inputs.
What the evidence says
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two independent data points, from two different vantage points, tell the same story.
S&P Global Market Intelligence, in its October 2025 report “Generative AI shows rapid growth but yields mixed results”, found that the share of companies abandoning most of their AI initiatives rose from 17% to 42% in one year, with the average organization scrapping 46% of its proof-of-concept projects before production. The survey covered 1,006 companies in North America and Europe, and the outcomes are self-reported, so read the figures as directional. Directionally, projects are not dying in the demo. They are dying on contact with real operating conditions.
Informatica’s CDO Insights 2025 survey of 600 data leaders names the condition. Data ties with technology at 43% as the top obstacle preventing GenAI initiatives from moving from pilot to production. And in the finding that should stop a budget meeting, 92% of surveyed data leaders are concerned that new AI pilots are proceeding without correcting the problems uncovered by previous initiatives. One scope note: this survey was commissioned by Informatica, a data management vendor, so treat it as directional too. The 92% figure is worth keeping anyway, because it describes a behavior, not a benchmark: companies know the foundation is broken and are funding the next project on it regardless.
Put the two surveys together and the picture is not a technology failing. It is a sequence failing, at scale, in public.
The order that works
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The companies that get value from automation run the steps in the opposite order from the companies that abandon it.
First they measure. What does the company already spend on AI, what has each initiative demonstrably produced, and which workflows would pay back fastest if automated well. This step is unglamorous and it is the one most organizations skip, because measuring feels like delay when a competitor just announced an AI initiative in a press release.
Then they organize. The data an automation depends on gets made current, documented, retrievable by someone who did not create it, and governed, before anything is built on it. The process gets defined precisely enough that an exception is a known category rather than a surprise. The knowledge that lives in one senior employee’s head gets written down while that employee still works there.
Then, and only then, they automate. At that point automation stops being a bet and becomes an execution of things already proven: a defined process, on organized data, with an owner and a number attached.
There is a one-line test hiding in this sequence. The technology you own minus the technology you actually use is lost business value nobody is measuring. Companies that automate first are adding to the owned side of that equation. Companies that measure and organize first are shrinking the gap between the two.
Three moves before your next automation project
Press enter or click to view image in full size

If an automation initiative is on your roadmap for this year, three things are worth doing before the kickoff, and the first two cost nothing.
First, write the process down as it actually runs today, exceptions included, and have the person who runs it confirm the write-up. If nobody can produce that document, the project is not an automation project yet. It is a definition project wearing an automation budget.
Second, list the data sources the workflow touches and answer one question per source: is this current, documented, and retrievable by someone who did not create it. Every “no” on that list is a pre-launch defect you are choosing to ship.
Third, score the wider foundation, not just this one workflow. A single automation can be rescued by heroics. A portfolio cannot. Knowing where the company stands across its AI spend, its data condition, and its past initiatives is what separates a deliberate second project from an accidental repeat of the first.
The 92% who fund the next pilot on an uncorrected foundation are not reckless people. They are busy people without a measurement in hand. The measurement is the cheap part.

The Executive AI ROI Scorecard is the five-minute version of that measurement: 20 scored statements across four areas, AI spend and return, visibility in AI-generated answers, data condition, and workflow change, with one calibration rule that keeps the number honest: score what is, not what is planned. Free, and your lowest section tells you what to fix before you automate anything: https://revenueexperts.ai/ai-roi-scorecard/
